Several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall tracked northeast across western Kentucky. These rounds of heavy rain produced localized flooding of roads, along with longer-term flooding along creeks and tributaries of the Ohio River. In the wake of a surface cold front, a persistent southwest flow of moist air aloft generated multiple rounds of widespread rain. Another cold front then moved east into western Kentucky on the morning of the 23rd. The three-day rainfall total at the Paducah National Weather Service office was 4.05 inches for the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of roads were flooded and closed in the river bottoms of the Clarks River and its tributaries. Water was over Kentucky Highway 131 at the Graves/McCracken County line. A secondary road was washed out at the intersection of Whitis and Watkins Road northeast of Hickory. Total road damage in Graves County was estimated over one million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 500 mb shortwave trough tracked east from the Plains, causing a surface low pressure center to develop northeast from Texas to the central Appalachians. Widespread wintry precipitation occurred as colder air filtered south on the back side of the low. Along and northwest of a line from Mayfield to Madisonville, one-tenth to one-quarter of an inch of ice glazed surfaces. Near the onset of precipitation, a period of sleet was reported. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. The automated observing station at the Paducah airport measured 0.23 inch of ice accretion. Most of the icing was on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces during the precipitation. There were some small tree limbs broken in the Paducah area. After the precipitation ended, temperatures fell through the 20's, causing icy spots to develop even on paved surfaces. Slick bridges and overpasses were common during the early morning hours, along with many vehicle accidents. Multiple crashes took place on Interstate 24 over the Cumberland River, which separates Lyon and Livingston Counties. Westbound traffic was backed up for several hours. An outbreak of severe thunderstorms and tornadoes occurred across western Kentucky. Most of the severe weather was along and south of a Paducah to Madisonville line. North of that line, serious flash flooding resulted in a fatality. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across western Kentucky. Instability was adequate for tornadoes and severe storms in the presence of very strong wind shear. A very moist air mass resulted in heavy rainfall, which produced localized flash flooding due to the water-logged ground. Locally 5 to 6 inches of rain had fallen over the previous week. Rainfall rates in these thunderstorms were locally one-half to one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief EF-1 tornado occurred in a very rural area of east central Graves County near the community of Golo. Near the touchdown location, some shingles were blown off a roof, and a few trees were uprooted or snapped. The tornado continued northeast and damaged trees along the path. The roof of a residence was uplifted briefly, and a window was blown in. Behind the residence, a mobile home with a wood base was lifted and rolled about 30 yards down a hill and was still intact. A small outbuilding was also destroyed on the property. Several large trees were uprooted or snapped on the property. The tornado track length was about one mile. Peak winds were estimated near 100 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-1 tornado crossed the southern part of the city of Murray, damaging about 40 homes. The tornado touched down several blocks west of Highway 641 and lifted on the east edge of the city at the Clarks River floodplain. Most of the damage consisted of roof damage, mainly shingle loss. Two homes were a total loss, with roof decking removed, windows blown out, and part of an exterior wall caved in due to projectile impacts. At least four businesses were damaged, with the majority of the roof removed from one. Dozens of trees were uprooted or broken. The mayor of Murray declared a state of emergency. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. Extensive flooding of low-lying bottomlands occurred. Numerous county roads were closed.

The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. At New Madrid, Missouri, the river crested at 41.4 feet on March 5, well above the flood stage of 34 feet. Bottomland roads and cropland were submerged. The cropland was mostly dormant this early in the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding continued on the Ohio River from late February. At the Smithland Lock and Dam, the river crested at 50.14 feet on the 2nd. Flood stage is 40 feet. This crest was a little over a foot lower than the Flood of March, 1997. Several homes were evacuated in the county. Numerous roads were flooded and closed, including several state roads. The flooding threatened some parts of Smithland, where a large sandbagging operation was conducted. Highway 137 was flooded and closed from Highway 60 through the Birdsville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Ohio River from late February. At the Paducah river gage, the river crested at 51.43 feet on the 2nd. Flood stage is 39 feet. This crest was just several inches lower than the Flood of March, 1997. A number of homes were evacuated in the county, including an area along Highway 1420 west of Paducah. This area is affected by backwater flooding of Massac Creek, which prompted the closure of Highways 1420 and 305. Numerous other roads were flooded and closed, mainly county roads. The floodgates were installed in the floodwall protecting the city of Paducah. Parks, roads, and boat ramps that were not protected by the floodwall were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued from late February on the Green River. Flooding began in lower Calhoun. A large portion of Rumsey was flooded. Voluntary evacuations of homes were recommended. Thousands of acres were flooded in the Island, Rumsey, and Jewel City areas, but the cropland was mostly dormant. High water signs were posted on Kentucky Highway 81 near the Green River bridge. Numerous county roads and a few other state roads were closed. The river crested at 31.67 feet early on the 1st. Flood stage is 23 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Ohio River. At J.T. Myers Lock and Dam near Uniontown, the river crested at 52.96 feet late on the evening of the 3rd. Flood stage is 37 feet. This crest was about one foot lower than the Flood of March, 1997. A small number of homes were evacuated. Numerous roads were closed, including 10 state highways. Highway 60 was covered by water near the Henderson County line, but the road remained open. Tributaries that flow into the Ohio River backed up, causing serious flooding miles away from the Ohio. Extensive flooding of cropland occurred, but agricultural damage was very limited because most crops were not yet planted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms moved along a warm front extending from central Missouri southeastward to the Tennessee Valley. Some storms produced large hail over far western Kentucky, west of Kentucky Lake. As the storms accelerated eastward towards cooler and more stable boundary layer air, they gradually lost intensity over the Kentucky Lake region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along the northern border of Graves County near Melber, golf ball sized hail was reported. The hail was mostly nickel to quarter sized, with golf ball hail mixed in. Multiple photos of the hail were posted on social media. The largest hailstones appeared to be a conglomeration of smaller stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance passed across Kentucky on March 17th. Scattered thunderstorms developed and quickly turned severe during the evening hours. Storms were responsible for bringing down trees and causing minor structural damage. Some hail reports of an inch were also reported.  Damage was confined to areas south of Mountain Parkway, from Lake Cumberland into southeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported shingles blown off of a roof in Barbourville.

The highest snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with isolated higher amounts, occurred northeast of an Owingsville to Prestonsburg line, due to a strong dome of cold air remaining in place on the southwestern extent of a high pressure system residing from Quebec into northeastern Kentucky.||Due to the strength of this low pressure system, thunderstorms developed across portions of south central to southeast Kentucky during the afternoon and evening. Pea sized hail was observed in London with temperatures only in the low to mid 40s! A rapidly deepening low off the Mid-Atlantic coast lead to very strong wind gusts on the 1st, and into the 2nd. As a trailing cold front moved through, a line of showers and thunderstorms developed, which also helped to mix down some of the stronger winds that were just off the surface.||The strong winds flipped the roof off a business in Ashland. The roof detached from an auto service business near the intersection of 13th Street and Central Avenue, and landed on 13th Street. Wind gusts of 35-45 mph were measured across northeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds flipped the roof off a business in Ashland. The roof detached from an auto service business near the intersection of 13th Street and Central Avenue. The roof landed on 13th Street. Wind gusts of 35-45 mph were measured across northeast Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty southwest winds occurred throughout western Kentucky. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 55 mph at numerous locations. The highest measured gust was 54 mph at the Paducah airport. Other measured gusts included 50 mph at Calvert City in Marshall County, 49 mph at a mesonet site near Cadiz, and 48 mph at a mesonet site near Marion. The Henderson and Owensboro airports both measured peak gusts of 46 mph. Tree limbs were blown down in isolated locations, including Calvert City. The strong gusty winds were associated with a surface low pressure system that tracked east-southeast from Iowa to the upper Ohio Valley during the afternoon.

Several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall in late February produced long-term flooding along the Clarks River, a tributary of the Ohio River. This flooding persisted until the Ohio River fell significantly and allowed backwater to drain. Flooding also occurred along the shores of Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley due to restricted water releases from the dams on both lakes. The lake levels were raised to hold back water from the Ohio and Tennessee Rivers, which were already above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding of the Clarks River continued from late February. A number of roads were flooded and closed in the river bottoms of the Clarks River and its tributaries. Water was over Kentucky Highway 131 at the Graves/McCracken County line. A secondary road was washed out at the intersection of Whitis and Watkins Road northeast of Hickory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance passed across Kentucky on March 17th. Scattered thunderstorms developed and quickly turned severe during the evening hours. Storms were responsible for bringing down trees and causing minor structural damage. Some hail reports of an inch were also reported.  Damage was confined to areas south of Mountain Parkway, from Lake Cumberland into southeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported two trees blown down onto power lines on Kentucky Highway 578 south of Annville.

A low pressure system crossed from southern Kentucky into southern West Virginia on the 12th. A mesoscale snow band set up just south of I-64, resulting in a very tight band of moderate to heavy snow. In Lawrence County, snowfall amounts ranged from around 2 inches across the north to 4-6 inches across the south.

An upper level low moved into the TN and OH Valley region and then into the Appalachians from Sunday evening, March 11th into Monday, March 12th. At the same time, a surface low pressure system tracked into the TN Valley and southern Appalachians before redeveloping over the Southeastern U.S. during the night and then off the coast of the Carolinas on Monday. Temperatures on Sunday the 11th were mild, reaching the upper 40s to mid 50s. However, the upper level low brought colder air into the region and was strong enough to allow rapid cooling of the atmosphere to near or just below the freezing mark in most locations in East KY between 11 PM EDT on March 11 and 3 AM EDT on March 12. The development of heavier snow bands occurred at the same time over central KY, and with the cooling moving into eastern KY overnight, affected portions of east central KY first. These bands then moved east and affected areas generally north of KY Highway 80 to near the Mountain Parkway corridor. Within the bands, snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour occurred, leading to an area of heavier amounts (see storm reports below). ||By 10 AM EDT on Monday, widespread accumulating snow had occurred over most of eastern Kentucky with the final band of snow working across the area. The snow finally exited into Virginia and West Virginia around 1 PM EDT. By that time, the heavy, wet snowfall totals ranged from as little as a half of an inch in some locations near the TN border to about 8 inches in areas near the Daniel Boone National Forest. A few localized higher amounts nearing 10 inches were observed on ridgetops from the Daniel Boone National Forest west. ||At the height of the storm, at least 12,000 customers in East KY were without power. Slick and slushy conditions led to a 40 vehicle pileup on Mountain Parkway in eastern Powell County, forcing the closure of the roadway between 1:30 AM and 2:30 AM EDT Sunday night. In addition to the falling snow, trees and limbs succumbed to the weight of the heavy wet snow and fell onto roadways. Although with temperatures near freezing, plowing and salting improved road conditions during the day on Monday. This process was slowed a bit as highway departments, first responders, and utility companies cleared snow, large limbs, and utility lines from roadways.||...Snowfall Reports from the Wet Snow Event An upper level low moved into the TN and OH Valley region and then into the Appalachians from Sunday evening, March 11th into Monday, March 12th. At the same time, a surface low pressure system tracked into the TN Valley and southern Appalachians before redeveloping over the Southeastern U.S. during the night and then off the coast of the Carolinas on Monday. Temperatures on Sunday the 11th were mild, reaching the upper 40s to mid 50s. However, the upper level low brought colder air into the region and was strong enough to allow rapid cooling of the atmosphere to near or just below the freezing mark in most locations in East KY between 11 PM EDT on March 11 and 3 AM EDT on March 12. The development of heavier snow bands occurred at the same time over central KY, and with the cooling moving into eastern KY overnight, affected portions of east central KY first. These bands then moved east and affected areas generally north of KY Highway 80 to near the Mountain Parkway corridor. Within the bands, snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour occurred, leading to an area of heavier amounts (see storm reports below). ||By 10 AM EDT on Monday, widespread accumulating snow had occurred over most of eastern Kentucky with the final band of snow working across the area. The snow finally exited into Virginia and West Virginia around 1 PM EDT. By that time, the heavy, wet snowfall totals ranged from as little as a half of an inch in some locations near the TN border to about 8 inches in areas near the Daniel Boone National Forest. A few localized higher amounts nearing 10 inches were observed on ridgetops from the Daniel Boone National Forest west. ||At the height of the storm, at least 12,000 customers in East KY were without power. Slick and slushy conditions led to a 40 vehicle pileup on Mountain Parkway in eastern Powell County, forcing the closure of the roadway between 1:30 AM and 2:30 AM EDT Sunday night. In addition to the falling snow, trees and limbs succumbed to the weight of the heavy wet snow and fell onto roadways. Although with temperatures near freezing, plowing and salting improved road conditions during the day on Monday. This process was slowed a bit as highway departments, first responders, and utility companies cleared snow, large limbs, and utility lines from roadways.||...Snowfall Reports from the Wet Snow Event Several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall in late February produced long-term flooding along the Clarks River, a tributary of the Ohio River. This flooding persisted until the Ohio River fell significantly and allowed backwater to drain. Flooding also occurred along the shores of Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley due to restricted water releases from the dams on both lakes. The lake levels were raised to hold back water from the Ohio and Tennessee Rivers, which were already above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Clarks River continued from late February. Several state roads were closed due to flooding along the Clarks River, which is a tributary of the Ohio River. Ohio River backwater was the main contributor to the flooding. Water was over Kentucky Highway 1255 (Bonds Road), Kentucky 131 near the Graves County line, Kentucky 787 near the Marshall County line, and Kentucky 284 between Reidland and Paducah. A few residences were threatened by rising water. Electrical power was cut to about a dozen homes on Kentucky 787.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall in late February produced long-term flooding along the Clarks River, a tributary of the Ohio River. This flooding persisted until the Ohio River fell significantly and allowed backwater to drain. Flooding also occurred along the shores of Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley due to restricted water releases from the dams on both lakes. The lake levels were raised to hold back water from the Ohio and Tennessee Rivers, which were already above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the lakeshore of Kentucky Lake inundated boat ramps, parking lots, and a few access roads. Some campsites were flooded at private campgrounds. The lake level rose to nearly 365 feet due to restricted water releases at Kentucky Dam. The water was held back to mitigate flooding on the Ohio River. The Kentucky Lake winter pool stage is 355 feet, and the normal summer pool stage is 359 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Ohio River from late February. A small number of homes were evacuated. Numerous roads were flooded and closed. The U.S. Highway 51/60/62 bridge connecting Wickliffe, KY and Cairo, IL was closed for about a week. This required motorists to use an eighty-mile long detour using the Interstate 24 bridge at Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. Low-lying bottomland parks, boat ramps, and some roads were flooded and closed. Water spilled across U.S. Highway 51 south of Wickliffe near the Carlisle County line, but the road was still open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued along the Ohio River from late February. The river crested very early in March. Tributaries such as the Tradewater River flooded extensively due to backwater flooding from the Ohio River. Flooding of the Tradewater River forced a couple of state roads to close. Countywide, numerous county roads and some state roads were closed. There were about ten state road closures. The Cave-In-Rock ferry connecting Illinois and Kentucky was closed due to flooding. Schools opened one hour late on some days during the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued along the Ohio River from late February. At the Owensboro river gage, the river crested at 47.3 feet on February 28th. Flood stage there is 40 feet. Panther Creek flooded extensively due to backwater flooding from the Ohio River, which forced isolated evacuations of residents in the area of Pettit. Panther Creek covered parts of U.S. Highway 431 and surrounded a few major businesses such as a home improvement store. Highway 81 was closed at the Panther Creek bridge. A downtown riverside park and parts of Water Street in Owensboro were flooded. Countywide, dozens of county roads and close to 12 state roads were closed. U.S. Highway 60 was closed near the Henderson County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. At New Madrid, Missouri, the river crested at 41.4 feet on March 5, well above the flood stage of 34 feet. A Hickman city police officer perished after attempting to drive through a flooded area on the night of March 2. The 45-year-old male was seen on the roof of his submerged patrol SUV shortly before being swept away with the vehicle. The victim and his vehicle were recovered from a flooded field early on the morning of March 3. Floodgates were raised at Hickman to protect the city. However, bottomland roads and cropland outside the floodwall were submerged. The cropland was mostly dormant this early in the season. Kentucky Highway 94 was closed in the bottomlands west of Hickman, along with several other state roads. The Dorena, MO to Hickman, KY ferry was closed due to the fast river currents and water over roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Ohio River. The river crested very early in March.  Tributary creeks flooded extensively due to backwater flooding from the Ohio River. Countywide, numerous county roads and some state roads were closed. U.S. Highway 60 was closed near the Spottsville bridge and the Daviess County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large mainstem rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The mainstem rivers such as the Ohio and Mississippi crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Green River from late February. Some local roads were closed, and considerable flooding of low-lying woods and fields occurred. The river crested in late February and fell slowly in early March.

A couple of clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms moved along a warm front extending from central Missouri southeastward to the Tennessee Valley. Some storms produced large hail over far western Kentucky, west of Kentucky Lake. As the storms accelerated eastward towards cooler and more stable boundary layer air, they gradually lost intensity over the Kentucky Lake region.

A surface low pressure center moved east from northern Arkansas to western Tennessee. North of the low pressure track, a few strong thunderstorms formed within a much larger area of showers.

A weak frontal boundary slid south to around the Ohio River Valley by mid-day. As diabatic heating increased and CAPE values reached into the 1500-2000 J/KG range, scattered showers and storms fired through the afternoon and evening. A shortwave rotated into the Great Lakes Region. Deep layer shear was weak and pulse storms with gusty winds caused wind damage. Slow storm motion and boundary collisions allowed for some locally heavy rainfall which caused some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One small tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow-moving low in the upper levels of the atmosphere caused snow to linger across the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky through much of the night. The upper-level low drifted east across the Ohio Valley. Snowfall amounts were generally from 1 to 2 inches along and east of a line from Henderson to Madisonville and Hopkinsville. Most of the snow accumulated on grassy areas. Paved surfaces were generally warm enough to melt the snow, however some icy spots developed as temperatures fell below freezing early on the 21st.

A mid to upper-level low moved southeast across the mid Mississippi Valley to the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. This induced the formation of a surface low pressure system over the southeastern states. A band of heavy snow formed across the lower Ohio Valley as colder air began to funnel into the area during the afternoon hours, changing the rain to snow rather quickly from northwest to southeast. Due to the warm ground and air temperatures at or above freezing, the snow had a hard time sticking on roads until it came down steadily. Visibility was reduced to one-half mile at times. North of a line from Paducah to Hopkinsville, amounts of 2 to 5 inches were common. There were even a couple of 6-inch reports just south and southwest of the Henderson/Owensboro area, where the heavy wet snow and gusty winds downed some tree limbs. Roads were in poor condition. Specific snowfall totals included: 6 inches at Sturgis (Union County) and Island (Mclean County), 4 inches at Owensboro and Whitesville (Daviess County), 4 inches near Greenville (Muhlenberg County), 2 inches at Madisonville, and 2 inches at Eddyville and Kuttawa (Lyon County). There was a sharp gradient of snowfall totals to the south. Very little snow fell from Paducah to Hopkinsville and points south.

Surface high pressure was centered over the Appalachian Mountains and a stacked low was over eastern Kansas. Weakly amplified mid-level flow funneled moisture up the Ohio River Valley from the Gulf of Mexico causing precipitable water values to go over 2 inches. This caused waves of heavy rainfall with some severe storm damage over multiple days in  central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew down, and a dryer was lifted and landed on a car.

Specific snowfall totals included: 6 inches at Sturgis (Union County) and Island (Mclean County), 4 inches at Owensboro and Whitesville (Daviess County), 4 inches near Greenville (Muhlenberg County), 2 inches at Madisonville, and 2 inches at Eddyville and Kuttawa (Lyon County). There was a sharp gradient of snowfall totals to the south. Very little snow fell from Paducah to Hopkinsville and points south.

A mid to upper-level low moved southeast across the mid Mississippi Valley to the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. This induced the formation of a surface low pressure system over the southeastern states. A band of heavy snow formed across the lower Ohio Valley as colder air began to funnel into the area during the afternoon hours, changing the rain to snow rather quickly from northwest to southeast. Due to the warm ground and air temperatures at or above freezing, the snow had a hard time sticking on roads until it came down steadily. Visibility was reduced to one-half mile at times. A slow-moving cold front drifted slowly southeast across western Kentucky on the 27th, then stalled over the Tennessee Valley on the 28th. The front returned north as a warm front and stalled over the Ohio Valley on the 29th. Widespread heavy rainfall from this oscillating front eventually resulted in isolated flooding of roads on the 29th. The Green River crept above flood stage at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor road flooding was reported along portions of Highways 279, 298, and 764. Signs were posted to advise motorists of water on the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green River rose above flood stage on the 29th and continued rising through the end of the month. The flooding during late March was minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the northern side of a low pressure system, a narrow band of strong forcing developed and dropped a significant area of snow across portions of eastern Kentucky on the 24th. Carter and Lawrence counties were right in the band, receiving 3 to 5 inches of snow. The highest snowfall accumulations were in Martha and Lowmansville, where 5 inches fell. Louisa and Olive Hill both reicieved 4.5 inches, with around 4 inches in Fallsburg, Webbville and Blaine.

A rare April snowfall was observed in western Kentucky. As surface low pressure moved from east Texas into Mississippi and Alabama, most precipitation on the afternoon and evening of April 6th stayed to the south. However, a secondary low developed and interacted with enough leftover moisture to produce a band of precipitation overnight into the morning hours on April 7th. Cold air funneled into the region through the night, allowing precipitation to quickly change over to snow, with a very brief transition period of sleet for some locations. Many locations picked up anywhere from 0.5 to 1.5 of snow. Impacts to travel were minimal due to warm pavement temperatures, which melted the snow on roadways. State highway personnel treated bridges and overpasses in some counties. The highest reported snow amount was 1.5 inches in the Paducah area and also at Mattoon in Crittenden County. This was the 1st measurable snow in the month of April since 1983 for some locations, including Paducah. In April of 2004, far southern portions of Kentucky from Murray to Hopkinsville were blanketed with light accumulations of snow.

An upper trough moving east of the Mississippi combined with high surface dew points in place over the Ohio Valley to generate a few severe thunderstorms in southern Indiana and central Kentucky. In addition to wind damage, brief tropical rainfall rates caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads in the area had water over them due to heavy rainfall, but no roads had to be closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a large house fire caused be lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell into the mid to upper 20's across most of western Kentucky. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Freeze damage from the back-to-back freezes was generally light. According to the Kentucky weekly crop report from the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the majority of winter wheat and alfalfa were not subjected to freeze damage. Winter wheat freeze damage was reported as 20 percent light and 78 percent experiencing none. Alfalfa freeze damage was reported as 45 percent light to moderate, and 54 percent experiencing none. Apple freeze damage was reported as 2 percent severe, 11 percent moderate, 31 percent light, and 56 percent with no damage. Peach freeze damage was reported as 4 percent severe, 13 percent moderate, 32 percent light, and 51 percent with no damage.

A strong early spring cold front led to a line of showers and thunderstorms developing across the Midwestern U.S. this afternoon and later pushing through eastern Kentucky during the late evening and overnight hours. Initially, a few embedded supercell thunderstorms produced large hail due to their rotation and stronger updrafts. Damaging wind gusts ended up being the primary threat across eastern Kentucky as these features became organized into line segments later into the night.||Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received across southeastern Kentucky where the greatest amount of instability was present. Homes in Leslie, Letcher, and Pike Counties suffered extensive damage from trees falling onto them, including one at Willow Tree Hollow near Marrowbone and Regina southeast of Pikeville that led to an injury to a person inside. More scattered wind damage was also reported up toward and north of Mountain Parkway as individual storm cells embedded within the larger line intensified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported penny to quarter sized hail near and north of Brodhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed south through the region during the evening of the 6th. In the cold air behind it, rain changed over to snow with some light accumulations occurring across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cincinnati airport CVG measured 0.6 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer in Burlington measured a half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread river flooding was observed on the Ohio River during the early and middle part of the month. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued because the heavy rainfall in March primed the ground for more flooding. Most of the flooding was minor, affecting low-lying woods and fields near the rivers. Many secondary roads in river bottomlands were flooded. Riverside parks were partially or completely flooded. Flooding of farmland was extensive, and farmers were forced to delay the planting of crops. Corn is normally planted by mid May.

A weak frontal boundary slid south to around the Ohio River Valley by mid-day. As diabatic heating increased and CAPE values reached into the 1500-2000 J/KG range, scattered showers and storms fired through the afternoon and evening. A shortwave rotated into the Great Lakes Region. Deep layer shear was weak and pulse storms with gusty winds caused wind damage. Slow storm motion and boundary collisions allowed for some locally heavy rainfall which caused some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down in different areas of Midway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread river flooding was observed on the Ohio River during the early and middle part of the month. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued because the heavy rainfall in March primed the ground for more flooding. Most of the flooding was minor, affecting low-lying woods and fields near the rivers. Many secondary roads in river bottomlands were flooded. Riverside parks were partially or completely flooded. Flooding of farmland was extensive, and farmers were forced to delay the planting of crops. Corn is normally planted by mid May.

A rare April snowfall was observed in western Kentucky. As surface low pressure moved from east Texas into Mississippi and Alabama, most precipitation on the afternoon and evening of April 6th stayed to the south. However, a secondary low developed and interacted with enough leftover moisture to produce a band of precipitation overnight into the morning hours on April 7th. Cold air funneled into the region through the night, allowing precipitation to quickly change over to snow, with a very brief transition period of sleet for some locations. Many locations picked up anywhere from 0.5 to 1.5 of snow. Impacts to travel were minimal due to warm pavement temperatures, which melted the snow on roadways. State highway personnel treated bridges and overpasses in some counties. The highest reported snow amount was 1.5 inches in the Paducah area and also at Mattoon in Crittenden County. This was the 1st measurable snow in the month of April since 1983 for some locations, including Paducah. In April of 2004, far southern portions of Kentucky from Murray to Hopkinsville were blanketed with light accumulations of snow.

A strong early spring cold front led to a line of showers and thunderstorms developing across the Midwestern U.S. this afternoon and later pushing through eastern Kentucky during the late evening and overnight hours. Initially, a few embedded supercell thunderstorms produced large hail due to their rotation and stronger updrafts. Damaging wind gusts ended up being the primary threat across eastern Kentucky as these features became organized into line segments later into the night.||Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received across southeastern Kentucky where the greatest amount of instability was present. Homes in Leslie, Letcher, and Pike Counties suffered extensive damage from trees falling onto them, including one at Willow Tree Hollow near Marrowbone and Regina southeast of Pikeville that led to an injury to a person inside. More scattered wind damage was also reported up toward and north of Mountain Parkway as individual storm cells embedded within the larger line intensified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a couple of trees blown down from Falcon to Salyersville, including one near Kentucky Highway 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary slid south to around the Ohio River Valley by mid-day. As diabatic heating increased and CAPE values reached into the 1500-2000 J/KG range, scattered showers and storms fired through the afternoon and evening. A shortwave rotated into the Great Lakes Region. Deep layer shear was weak and pulse storms with gusty winds caused wind damage. Slow storm motion and boundary collisions allowed for some locally heavy rainfall which caused some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Green River into mid April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, the excessive rainfall in March primed the ground for more flooding in April. The flooding impacted mainly agricultural low-lying areas, which resulted in a delay in the planting of spring crops. Corn is normally planted by mid May.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved west around a surface low pressure center near the Alabama/Georgia border. A cold air funnel cloud was photographed in association with one of the strongest thunderstorms. Some of the storms were very slow movers, which resulted in isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The funnel cloud was observed just off U.S. Highway 62 by numerous individuals.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain sent Mayfield Creek out of its banks. Kentucky Highway 339 was closed at the bridge over the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures fell into the upper 20's across western Kentucky. The cold weather was accompanied by a coating of snow and some gusty winds. Fruit growers had expected that temperatures in the 20's would cause damage. Some nurseries at big box retailers had begun selling warm weather flowering plants and vegetables. Many ornamental trees and shrubs were in flower except for dogwoods and azaleas.

Fruit growers had expected that temperatures in the 20's would cause damage. Some nurseries at big box retailers had begun selling warm weather flowering plants and vegetables. Many ornamental trees and shrubs were in flower except for dogwoods and azaleas.

For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell into the mid to upper 20's across most of western Kentucky. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Freeze damage from the back-to-back freezes was generally light. According to the Kentucky weekly crop report from the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the majority of winter wheat and alfalfa were not subjected to freeze damage. Winter wheat freeze damage was reported as 20 percent light and 78 percent experiencing none. Alfalfa freeze damage was reported as 45 percent light to moderate, and 54 percent experiencing none. Apple freeze damage was reported as 2 percent severe, 11 percent moderate, 31 percent light, and 56 percent with no damage. Peach freeze damage was reported as 4 percent severe, 13 percent moderate, 32 percent light, and 51 percent with no damage.

According to the Kentucky weekly crop report from the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the majority of winter wheat and alfalfa were not subjected to freeze damage. Winter wheat freeze damage was reported as 20 percent light and 78 percent experiencing none. Alfalfa freeze damage was reported as 45 percent light to moderate, and 54 percent experiencing none. Apple freeze damage was reported as 2 percent severe, 11 percent moderate, 31 percent light, and 56 percent with no damage. Peach freeze damage was reported as 4 percent severe, 13 percent moderate, 32 percent light, and 51 percent with no damage.

The peak wind gust at the Mayfield airport was 44 mph. In Todd County, a tree limb about six inches in diameter was blown down in Guthrie.

Surface high pressure was centered over the Appalachian Mountains and a stacked low was over eastern Kansas. Weakly amplified mid-level flow funneled moisture up the Ohio River Valley from the Gulf of Mexico causing precipitable water values to go over 2 inches. This caused waves of heavy rainfall with some severe storm damage over multiple days in  central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that six to eight inches of flowing water was flowing over highway 101 and US 31W.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Highway 261 and over much of the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring cold front led to a line of showers and thunderstorms developing across the Midwestern U.S. this afternoon and later pushing through eastern Kentucky during the late evening and overnight hours. Initially, a few embedded supercell thunderstorms produced large hail due to their rotation and stronger updrafts. Damaging wind gusts ended up being the primary threat across eastern Kentucky as these features became organized into line segments later into the night.||Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received across southeastern Kentucky where the greatest amount of instability was present. Homes in Leslie, Letcher, and Pike Counties suffered extensive damage from trees falling onto them, including one at Willow Tree Hollow near Marrowbone and Regina southeast of Pikeville that led to an injury to a person inside. More scattered wind damage was also reported up toward and north of Mountain Parkway as individual storm cells embedded within the larger line intensified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highway officials reported numerous trees blown down from Talcum to southeast of Hindman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station northwest of Harlan recorded a 62 mph thunderstorm wind gust. Farther east in Evarts, a beauty salon suffered roof damage due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved west around a surface low pressure center near the Alabama/Georgia border. A cold air funnel cloud was photographed in association with one of the strongest thunderstorms. Some of the storms were very slow movers, which resulted in isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water covered portions of numerous roads. At least one vehicle became stranded in floodwaters, prompting a response from fire and rescue personnel. Rainfall totals over the previous 36 hours were up to 6 inches in spots. At the mesonet site 6 miles southwest of Mayfield, the rainfall total for the 22nd and 23rd was 5.04 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought more showers and thunderstorms to the region early on the 14th. This rain fell on ground that was very moist from the excessive rainfall in March. As a result, rapid runoff caused isolated road flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest of Murray, a secondary road was flooded between Kentucky Highways 121 and 80. Travel was not advisable in the flooded area.

A weak frontal boundary slid south to around the Ohio River Valley by mid-day. As diabatic heating increased and CAPE values reached into the 1500-2000 J/KG range, scattered showers and storms fired through the afternoon and evening. A shortwave rotated into the Great Lakes Region. Deep layer shear was weak and pulse storms with gusty winds caused wind damage. Slow storm motion and boundary collisions allowed for some locally heavy rainfall which caused some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down near Scott and Vine in Paris. Two other trees were reported down near 833 Brentsville Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 2 miles west of Augusta measured an inch of snow, as did the cooperative observer in Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 14, lightning from thunderstorms in the area struck a tree and killed 31 sheep on a farm in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that a lightning strike hit a nearby tree on a private farm that killed 31 sheep. In addition, portions of fencing was heavily damaged by the strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley on April 3. Ahead of it, very warm and moist air surged into the region with highs around 80 degrees. As a cold front swept through, lines of strong to severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind gusts and two short-lived tornadoes to central Kentucky. There were several pockets of intense straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a measured gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms moved eastward out of southern Illinois and southeast Missouri. The storms produced four tornadoes over the far western counties of Kentucky before weakening as they moved east across the Kentucky Lake region. The most significant tornado, which occurred over Livingston County, was rated EF-2. The severe storms occurred ahead of a cold front that accelerated east-southeast toward the lower Ohio Valley. Cooling aloft ahead of a shortwave trough over the Plains, combined with diurnal heating and moist southerly winds, resulted in a corridor of  convection ahead of the front. The storms weakened somewhat as they moved east across the Kentucky Lake region into slightly drier and more stable air.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two miles SW on Hodgenville on Old Sonora Road a large 36x30 foot well built metal barn sustained significant structural damage when the barn broke free from the foundation. Portions of the trusses and 6x6 poles were found 100 yards to the east. Several large pieces of metal roofing and siding were blown eastward up to 1/2 mile and several splintered sections of the roof were impaled in the ground nearby. There was sporadic wind damage eastward from this point and then a concentrated area of damage at Larue County Middle School. Wind gusts lifted a section of the cafeteria roof and threw it about 25 feet downwind to the east. About 100 yards further east from the school, a small storage shed was pushed into the dugout dislodging several concrete blocks. Winds around the school area were estimated to be around 65 mph. Northeast of Hodgenville in Ovsen Heights, a concentrated area of wind brought down several power poles and trees knocking down a few outbuildings and back deck areas. Winds were estimated to be 65 to 70 mph at this location.

The tornado did some damage to a grain silo along with some tree damage. An antique horse sled was picked up and moved about 10 feet. ||The tornado briefly lifted in a valley before striking a residence on the east side of Highway 1822 and lifting the roof off a family residence. Insulation from the roof was thrown eastward and also rotated back and covered the back of the house. ||Further east on Webster Rd, another one story family residence sustained significant roof, gutter, and siding damage. The owners hoop barn was pushed in violently. There were pine trees snapped. ||The NWS would like to thank emergency management director Mike Wilder as well as Garrard County ARES director Jim Boyd for their assistance.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in a very rural area along Highway 763, just north of Dyer Hill. A few outbuildings were destroyed.

The storms produced four tornadoes over the far western counties of Kentucky before weakening as they moved east across the Kentucky Lake region. The most significant tornado, which occurred over Livingston County, was rated EF-2. The severe storms occurred ahead of a cold front that accelerated east-southeast toward the lower Ohio Valley. Cooling aloft ahead of a shortwave trough over the Plains, combined with diurnal heating and moist southerly winds, resulted in a corridor of  convection ahead of the front. The storms weakened somewhat as they moved east across the Kentucky Lake region into slightly drier and more stable air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was measured on Interstate 24 at Kentucky Route 695, just southwest of Hopkinsville. In downtown Hopkinsville, part of the roof was blown off a bakery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell into the mid to upper 20's across most of western Kentucky. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Freeze damage from the back-to-back freezes was generally light. According to the Kentucky weekly crop report from the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the majority of winter wheat and alfalfa were not subjected to freeze damage. Winter wheat freeze damage was reported as 20 percent light and 78 percent experiencing none. Alfalfa freeze damage was reported as 45 percent light to moderate, and 54 percent experiencing none. Apple freeze damage was reported as 2 percent severe, 11 percent moderate, 31 percent light, and 56 percent with no damage. Peach freeze damage was reported as 4 percent severe, 13 percent moderate, 32 percent light, and 51 percent with no damage.

An upper trough moving east of the Mississippi combined with high surface dew points in place over the Ohio Valley to generate a few severe thunderstorms in southern Indiana and central Kentucky. In addition to wind damage, brief tropical rainfall rates caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of trees down around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the second day of heavy rain across central Kentucky, a quasi-stationary boundary was draped across the area.  Training storms across south central KY in the morning resulted in significant flash flooding. Strong to severe thunderstorms then developed during the late morning across east central KY. As these storms moved off to the east another round of storms moved in from the west in response to a system moving out of central Missouri. These storms resulted in many reports of wind damage as well as some additional flooding and flash flooding. Moderate shear contributed to an EF-1 tornado  touching down in Louisville near the Ford truck plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reportedly flowing across Lone Star Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked from 110 to 115 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index was from 105 to 110 on the 4th, and near 105 on the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the mid to upper 90's. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the second day of heavy rain across central Kentucky, a quasi-stationary boundary was draped across the area.  Training storms across south central KY in the morning resulted in significant flash flooding. Strong to severe thunderstorms then developed during the late morning across east central KY. As these storms moved off to the east another round of storms moved in from the west in response to a system moving out of central Missouri. These storms resulted in many reports of wind damage as well as some additional flooding and flash flooding. Moderate shear contributed to an EF-1 tornado  touching down in Louisville near the Ford truck plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was water over Ebenezer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary surface boundary remained draped across the region from the previous day.  An upper level short wave associated with a departing trough swung through the region yielding another round of convection to south-central Kentucky. Surface winds veered back to the south/southwest and allowed rich low-level moisture to push back into the region.  Despite the cloudy conditions, heating lead to decent instability across southern Kentucky during the afternoon.  Modest bulk shear was in place once again. Clusters and short line segments formed moving from west to east causing tree damage along their path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few trees down in the city of Glasgow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down at the entrance of Westport Woods Apartments in Jefferson County.  After exiting the Forest Spring North subdivision, the tornado lifted briefly before dropping back down on the east side of Pewee Valley near Five Forks Drive.  Near Manassas Drive a trampoline was thrown 60 yards and a large sycamore tree was uprooted next to an air conditioner that was thrown to the north. The tornado then increased in strength and uprooted several trees along Hawley Gibson Road. One resident lost twenty softwood and hardwood trees. The tornado lifted just east of Hawley Gibson Road, twisting several trees on a farm there.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down at the entrance of Westport Woods Apartments. Across from the complex numerous trees were snapped or topped off. On pacelli Place parts of trees were thrown into two houses, puncturing the siding. Most of the damage was high end EF0 around 85mph with much of the damage being 20 to 50 yards off the ground.||There were some hardwood trees uprooted and parts of them fell onto homes, fences, trampolines, and swing sets. Besides trees being twisted and turned in multiple directions, there was leaf and mud spattering on several homes in opposite directions of the storm movement. Intermittent tree top damage continued near Lake Louisvilla. At Westport Business  Center there was significant roof damage with portions of the roofing material and parts of the roof supports 20 to 50 yards downwind and several windows blown out. At Ceva Logistics a large section of the north wall was pushed out due to 75-80 mph winds.||The tornado struck the north portion of Westport Bend Commerce Park, snapping and bending several trees. The tornado began to skip and struck a few trees in Forest Springs North before moving into Oldham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave passing to the south of central Kentucky caused isolated storms in the predawn hours of June 24th. Rounds of storms developed along a nearly stationary surface boundary from late morning into the afternoon on June 25th. Some minor flooding occurred with repeated rainfall, and isolated cells produced severe weather across central Kentucky, including two small tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief tornado did most of its |damage at treetop level, snapping|the trunks of or uprooting at least |two dozen large, mature oaks and cedars, |and causing limb damage to many others. |In one case, it snapped the 2-foot |diameter trunk of a large cedar less than |5 feet above the ground, but lofted the |tree over nearby utility lines - which |remained intact - and deposited the tree |200 feet to the east. ||The tornado did occasionally reach closer |to the ground, causing significant damage |to two structures. The first was a large |brick ranch home, which had most of the |north half of its roof torn off, and |insulation spread eastward in a narrow |path. One piece of roofing lumber was |found 500 feet ENE of the home. A second |building, a large 2-story brick industrial |building 1/2 mile east - had about 20 |percent of its metal roof peeled off. |Immediately after hitting this building |and downing trees in a nearby cemetery, |it crossed U.S. Hwy 62 and did additional |tree damage for another several hundred |feet as it moved through a wooded area. |The National Weather Service thanks Ohio |County Emergency Management for their |assistance in this survey, including |drone surveillance that helped |identify the path of this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms moved west/southwest from West Virginia into portions of northeast and east Kentucky this evening. Due to the high amount of moisture and subsequent instability in place, the downdraft portion of one of these storms produced winds of up to 85 mph as it collapsed over Paintsville. This resulted in substantial damage in Paintsville, including half of a roof being removed from an office building along with the steeple of a church being blown off. A couple of residential buildings were also damaged from falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey confirmed microburst winds of between 75-85 mph felled several trees east of Paintsville, including one which fell on a residential building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This weak tornado skipped along mainly at |treetop level near the Moutardier Recreation |Area about 1/2 mile south of the Edmonson-|Grayson County Line.  The tornado snapped the |upper reaches of several tree trunks near its|initial point near several homes - leaving |debris in a circular pattern - then skipped|eastward, causing other minor tree damage |before dropping down to become a waterspout |on Nolin Lake northeast of the Moutardier|Marina.  The waterspout was captured on video |and shared on social media, confirming the |existence of the rotation and funnel.  The|waterspout dissipated as it approached the |east shore of the channel.

The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked from 110 to 115 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index was from 105 to 110 on the 4th, and near 105 on the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the mid to upper 90's. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the second day of heavy rain across central Kentucky, a quasi-stationary boundary was draped across the area.  Training storms across south central KY in the morning resulted in significant flash flooding. Strong to severe thunderstorms then developed during the late morning across east central KY. As these storms moved off to the east another round of storms moved in from the west in response to a system moving out of central Missouri. These storms resulted in many reports of wind damage as well as some additional flooding and flash flooding. Moderate shear contributed to an EF-1 tornado  touching down in Louisville near the Ford truck plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down on State Road 1073.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the second day of heavy rain across central Kentucky, a quasi-stationary boundary was draped across the area.  Training storms across south central KY in the morning resulted in significant flash flooding. Strong to severe thunderstorms then developed during the late morning across east central KY. As these storms moved off to the east another round of storms moved in from the west in response to a system moving out of central Missouri. These storms resulted in many reports of wind damage as well as some additional flooding and flash flooding. Moderate shear contributed to an EF-1 tornado  touching down in Louisville near the Ford truck plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AGC Automotive Americas had mechanical units blown off of their roof and metal wall panels blown approximately 1,000 to 1,500 feet downwind.

South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern to central Kentucky this afternoon, morphing into a large complex during the evening. These formed along a stationary frontal boundary and in the vicinity of the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Gordon.  The slow movement of these led to flash flooding in Bath County, while the strength of one storm briefly increased enough to result in a few trees down west of West Liberty. In addition, a cluster of thunderstorms developed along the Kentucky and Tennessee state line. This moved northwest and intensified such that a couple of large storage buildings were demolished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways officials reported a few trees blown down on Kentucky Highway 705 west of West Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms produced wind damage across portions of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of July 20, 2018, extending into the early morning hours of July 21. The hardest hit areas were in Montgomery County, where Emergency Management reported that 47 roads were closed at one point due to downed trees and power lines resulting in nearly 4,600 power outages. Additionally, a disaster declaration was made for the county as multiple buildings sustained damage. These included a tobacco warehouse that had its roof blown off as well as��a hotel in Mount Sterling whose sign and building both sustained damage from severe straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a tree blown down across Poley Ellis Lane east of Pine Knot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One cell in a line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved through Knott County this afternoon. Minor damage was sustained just east of Hindman, while additional damage occurred near Meta in Pike County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree blown down along Kentucky Highway 194 in Meta.

A multiple day period of rainfall led to flooding and flash flooding across portions of southeast and far eastern Kentucky on this day. A cold front, which pushed into eastern Kentucky on September 26, stalled across southeast Kentucky and oozed back toward the northwest today. This brought another widespread round of 1-2 inches of rain across eastern Kentucky, with amounts exceeding 2.5 inches in Floyd and Harlan Counties. Along with numerous creeks and streams overspilling their banks and impacting roadways, several mudslides were induced by saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media and dispatch relayed reports of nearly 3 feet of standing water in a poor drainage area on Duffield Street in Harlan. Farther southwest of Harlan, runoff caused Little Creek in Crawford Branch to develop a swift enough current to wash out a culvert on a driveway on Kentucky Highway 2425.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of oppressive heat and humidity were experienced across parts of west Kentucky. The extreme western end of the state was impacted the most. From Paducah south and west, heat indices peaked in the triple digits on five consecutive days. The highest heat indices were observed on the 14th, when values from 110 to 115 were recorded. Areas of northwest Kentucky including Owensboro and Henderson were impacted the least. At Owensboro and Henderson, heat indices peaked around 105 degrees on the 14th. Otherwise, heat index readings generally stayed below 100 in northwest Kentucky. High pressure over the Great Lakes region pushed a cold front down into the lower Ohio Valley. Heat indices remained persistently dangerous ahead of the cold front, while slightly lower values infiltrated northwest Kentucky.

Showers and thunderstorms formed across the middle Ohio River Valley on the 20th as a warm front lifted through. The showers and storms eventually organized into a line which moved through northeastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening. Sub-severe wind gusts caused isolated tree damage, the maximum wind gusts across the area were generally 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch was blown down along Woodland Avenue. The tree fell on a power line and brought it down too.

A strong thunderstorm increased in intensity and became severe as it moved out of central Kentucky into the Mountain Parkway corridor of eastern Kentucky. A narrow swath of wind damage occurred between Clay City and Stanton in Powell County, prior to the storm quickly diminishing in strength east of Stanton this evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of wind damage were received from a National Weather Service employee, trained storm spotter, local fire rescue, and dispatch in between Clay City and Stanton. This consisted of a plethora of trees blown down, resulting in several power outages. The roofs of a barn and mobile home were also damaged as this storm moved east along Mountain Parkway.

From Paducah south and west, heat indices peaked in the triple digits on five consecutive days. The highest heat indices were observed on the 14th, when values from 110 to 115 were recorded. Areas of northwest Kentucky including Owensboro and Henderson were impacted the least. At Owensboro and Henderson, heat indices peaked around 105 degrees on the 14th. Otherwise, heat index readings generally stayed below 100 in northwest Kentucky. High pressure over the Great Lakes region pushed a cold front down into the lower Ohio Valley. Heat indices remained persistently dangerous ahead of the cold front, while slightly lower values infiltrated northwest Kentucky.

A rapidly developing convective cell produced a brief waterspout. The developing thunderstorm occurred ahead of a weak cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over southwest Ohio. A closed 500 mb low over east central Missouri provided additional support for the activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout over Kentucky Lake was captured on video by at least one boater. The rope-like funnel took a long, winding path from the cloud base to the water. A small but intense cloud of spray marked the touchdown point. The waterspout moved into Duncan Bay, where it appeared to dissipate near the forested shoreline of the Land Between The Lakes National Recreation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated and slow moving severe thunderstorm moved across portions of Martin County on July 1st, 2018. The storm blew down numerous trees in the Turkey Creek area northeast of Inez, resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported numerous trees were blown down in the Turkey Creek area of Kentucky Highway 908 northeast of Inez. As a result, several power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms moved west/southwest from West Virginia into portions of northeast and east Kentucky this evening. Due to the high amount of moisture and subsequent instability in place, the downdraft portion of one of these storms produced winds of up to 85 mph as it collapsed over Paintsville. This resulted in substantial damage in Paintsville, including half of a roof being removed from an office building along with the steeple of a church being blown off. A couple of residential buildings were also damaged from falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey confirmed microburst winds of between 75-85 mph removed half of a roof off of an office building and a steeple off of a church building in Paintsville. Several trees were also blown down, including one onto a residential building. A portion of Kentucky Highway 321/40 was shut down for a period of time due to downed and damaged trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across portions of eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of July 5, 2018. The worst damage occurred in northeastern Frenchburg in Menifee County, where several hardwood trees were uprooted and a house had a portion of its metal roofing peeled back. London and Stanton also sustained wind damage as trees were blown down and hail up to the size of quarters fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter and a local media outlet reported trees down from Maple Street in Stanton south to Mountain Parkway.

Isolated thunderstorms developed as the atmosphere became strongly unstable during the afternoon and early evening hours. Mixed layer capes were near 3000 j/kg. The storms drifted slowly south and southwest around a strong upper level high centered over the Missouri Valley. An isolated damaging wind gust was observed with the strongest cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down over a roadway in the city limits of Clinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over far west Kentucky as strong solar heating contributed to a rather unstable atmosphere by midday. A few storms produced very localized wind damage. A moist west-southwest low level wind around 20 knots at 850 mb provided ample moisture for isolated minor flooding with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was reported at a grocery store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across portions of eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of July 5, 2018. London and Stanton also sustained wind damage as trees were blown down and hail up to the size of quarters fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a microburst, estimated at 100 to 110 mph, felled numerous trees and large branches in and just northeast of Frenchburg. The damage began just east of Kentucky Highway 36 near the water treatment plant, and ended east of Highway 36 near a salt storage structure, where the thick tarp covering of the structure was damaged. Some damage was also noted just west of Highway 36, including downed power lines resulting in the closure of the highway for several hours. Some shingles were blown off of a small shed, and metal roofing was peeled off of the roof of a house. The most intense damage occurred along a 125 yard stretch just south of Sorrell Road to Beaver Creek, where estimated winds of 100 to 110 mph broke trunks and uprooted several hardwood trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms produced wind damage across portions of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of July 20, 2018, extending into the early morning hours of July 21. The hardest hit areas were in Montgomery County, where Emergency Management reported that 47 roads were closed at one point due to downed trees and power lines resulting in nearly 4,600 power outages. Additionally, a disaster declaration was made for the county as multiple buildings sustained damage. These included a tobacco warehouse that had its roof blown off as well as��a hotel in Mount Sterling whose sign and building both sustained damage from severe straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed two trees blown down southwest of Hollyhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm moved east-southeast across the southwest corner of Kentucky during the evening. This storm produced large hail. The storm occurred in a very unstable environment ahead of a cold front. This cold front extended from southern Indiana and Illinois into southeast Missouri and northern Arkansas. As the front shifted south, the main 500 mb shortwave over Missouri dove southeastward across the lower Mississippi Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multiple day period of rainfall led to flooding and flash flooding across portions of southeast and far eastern Kentucky on this day. A cold front, which pushed into eastern Kentucky on September 26, stalled across southeast Kentucky and oozed back toward the northwest today. This brought another widespread round of 1-2 inches of rain across eastern Kentucky, with amounts exceeding 2.5 inches in Floyd and Harlan Counties. Along with numerous creeks and streams overspilling their banks and impacting roadways, several mudslides were induced by saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported a mudslide on U.S. Highway 460 outside of Pikeville.

From Paducah south and west, heat indices peaked in the triple digits on five consecutive days. The highest heat indices were observed on the 14th, when values from 110 to 115 were recorded. Areas of northwest Kentucky including Owensboro and Henderson were impacted the least. At Owensboro and Henderson, heat indices peaked around 105 degrees on the 14th. Otherwise, heat index readings generally stayed below 100 in northwest Kentucky. High pressure over the Great Lakes region pushed a cold front down into the lower Ohio Valley. Heat indices remained persistently dangerous ahead of the cold front, while slightly lower values infiltrated northwest Kentucky.

A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported the entrance to apartment complex flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed this afternoon ahead of an upper level disturbance, bringing moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into eastern Kentucky. Isolated wind damage occurred in Laurel County as one storm initially grew very strong. Additional storms produced penny to nickel sized hail, while portions of northeast Letcher County experienced flash flooding due to persistent storms producing between 2-3 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch and local media reported several instances of flash flooding from near Fish Pond Lake to Fleming-Neon and Whitaker. This included portions of Kentucky Highway 3406 being closed, along with several other local roads. Just southeast of Tolliver Town and near Payne Gap, a couple of culverts were displaced due to swiftly flowing flood waters. Three people had to be rescued from their vehicles after they became inundated by water, while four homes were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the warmth of the afternoon. A weak mid-level shortwave trough over the Ozarks helped initiate convection. Although the air mass was only marginally unstable, thunderstorms were able to organize within a veering and modestly strengthening wind profile. Deep layer bulk wind shear was around 30 to 35 knots. Multicell storm clusters produced isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel-size hail was reported near the intersection of U.S. Highway 41 and the Hopkinsville bypass.
