A cluster of strong thunderstorms moved out of south central Kentucky into the Lake Cumberland region from late in the evening on May 10th through the early overnight hours of May 11th. One of these storms produced up to quarter sized hail near Bronston in Pulaski County, just to the south of Somerset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was observed on Slate Branch Road south of Somerset. The report was received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of KY 1153, 765, and 3201 closed due to high water and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted the region on the last day of May. Damaging winds, large hail, and flooding were observed in this outbreak. The first round of storms moved through northwest Kentucky, mainly north of Madisonville, during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Locally damaging winds were associated with this line of storms. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. The first round of storms left an outflow boundary across southeast Missouri and the lower Ohio Valley, where scattered thunderstorms developed. Surface dewpoints near the boundary were in the lower 70s, which contributed to moderately strong instability. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the late day and evening round of storms. Mid-level winds around 50 knots located just ahead of the shortwave helped to create moderate to strong deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water was over Highway 41 near the junction of Old Kelly Road. A rural back road northwest of Hopkinsville was impassable due to flooding. A trained spotter reported 1.89 inches of rain fell in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain associated with the remains of Sub-tropical Storm Alberto produced locally up to 4 to 6 inches of rain in far western Kentucky. The center of the storm made landfall on the Florida peninsula on the 28th and continued moving north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was partially over U.S. Highway 62 on the west side of Paducah. Fast-moving floodwater from Champion Creek was flowing over Highway 994.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow-moving warm front moved northeast across western Kentucky during the daytime hours. The air mass along and behind the front was moist and unstable, setting the stage for locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightly-travelled rural road northeast of Barlow was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of the 10th as a front moved through. One of these storms became rather strong, producing some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree and several limbs were blown down in Central Park in Ashland. Another tree was blown down along Sucks Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed on the afternoon of the 21st as a warm front lifted across the middle Ohio River Valley. One of the storms produced wind damage and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and snow melt upstream led to significant rises on area rivers in the middle of the month. Portions of the Ohio River went into flood starting in the middle of the month, remaining above flood stage into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at McAlpine Upper reached the 23' Flood Stage at 1500 EST on February 19.  It exceeded the 30' Moderate Flood Stage between 1345 EST on February 23 and continued in Moderate Flood into March.  The river crested at 35.64' on February 26th, it's highest mark since 1997, and the 10th highest stage on record for the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and snow melt upstream led to significant rises on area rivers in the middle of the month. Portions of the Ohio River went into flood starting in the middle of the month, remaining above flood stage into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at McAlpine Lower reached the 55' Flood Stage a 0700 EST on February 20.  It exceeded the 65' Moderate Flood Stage between 0415 EST on February 25 and 0415 EST on February 28, having crested at 67.13' on February 26, making it the 12th highest crest on record.  The gauge remained above flood into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rainfall and snow melt upstream led to rises on area rivers in the Green River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drakes Creek near Alvaton went into minor flood. The river crested at 25.55 feet which is 1.55 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As subtropical storm Alberto moved into the Mississippi Valley, strong thunderstorms formed in the outer bands of the storm over the middle Ohio River Valley on the 30th. As the remnants moved towards the Great Lakes, additional showers and storms moved through on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Kroger sign was blown down. Winds with this storm were estimated to be around 50 mph.

The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads closed due to high water and flooding.

The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. EVENT_NARRATIVE: KY 2885 closed at mile 2.6 due to high water and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed this afternoon, extending into the evening ahead of an upper level disturbance. While storms were progressive in nature, continued development and repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall over certain locations led to instances of flash flooding. The heavy rainfall also caused a mudslide which damaged a mobile home in Perry County. Additionally, a cloud to ground lightning strike near Somerset set a hay barn on fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a mudslide near Leatherwood, resulting in damage to a trailer below the falling mud and water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted the region on the last day of May. Damaging winds, large hail, and flooding were observed in this outbreak. The first round of storms moved through northwest Kentucky, mainly north of Madisonville, during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Locally damaging winds were associated with this line of storms. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. The first round of storms left an outflow boundary across southeast Missouri and the lower Ohio Valley, where scattered thunderstorms developed. Surface dewpoints near the boundary were in the lower 70s, which contributed to moderately strong instability. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the late day and evening round of storms. Mid-level winds around 50 knots located just ahead of the shortwave helped to create moderate to strong deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported along the Hancock County line.

A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported trees down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of severe thunderstorms moved southeast, producing locally damaging winds and hail. The storms occurred along a warm front that extended from central Illinois southeast into northwest Kentucky. The storms occurred during the warmest and most unstable time of day, when surface-based capes were near 2,000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of damaging winds extended from the west side of Owensboro to a few miles south of the city. A metal roof was blown off a cable company building on the northwest side of the city. About a mile west of Owensboro, a trained spotter estimated a wind gust to 60 mph. A few miles south of Owensboro, a wind gust to 60 mph was measured by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed north of a closed mid-level low over northern Mississippi and western Tennessee. During the warmest and most unstable time of day, one of the storms downed a couple of tree limbs. Surface-based capes peaked around 2,000 j/kg as the storms arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of large tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough over the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys promoted the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some locally heavy rain caused isolated flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two vehicles were stranded in floodwater in rural areas south of Owensboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather system set up as an area of low pressure pushed across the Tennessee Valley on Saturday, May 5, 2018. Initially, a few supercell-like thunderstorms produced large hail due to their rotation and stronger updrafts near the Virginia border. A bowing line of storms then brought damaging wind gusts into the Lake Cumberland region later in the afternoon. There were multiple reports of large hail in some of the stronger storms in southeast Kentucky, with the largest hail occurring in Harlan County near Loyall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of quarter to tennis ball sized hail were received via social media and local Emergency Management, from northwest of Harlan to Baxter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system, in combination with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Gordon, and abundant moisture brought a long duration heavy rainfall event to much of the Bluegrass region of eastern Kentucky. Southwesterly low level winds ahead of a very slow moving cold front ushered rich moisture into eastern Kentucky, which when combined with the available lift, resulted in copious amounts of rainfall. This, in combination with several inches of rain the past couple of days, led to widespread flooding and flash flooding as multi-day rainfall amounts of 3-6 inches occurred with isolated totals near 7-8 inches.||Bath, Montgomery, and Menifee Counties all saw significant impacts. This includes 50-70 homes and 20-25 businesses, one of which was a school in Menifee County, being inundated with water. Numerous roads were damaged as culverts were washed out, forcing schools to close the following day. Disaster declarations were made for Montgomery and Menifee Counties while nearly a dozen water rescues were performed. Additionally, a nine year old boy was swept into a culvert in Morehead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few inches of water was observed flowing across Spencer Road southeast of Mount Sterling.

This, in combination with several inches of rain the past couple of days, led to widespread flooding and flash flooding as multi-day rainfall amounts of 3-6 inches occurred with isolated totals near 7-8 inches.||Bath, Montgomery, and Menifee Counties all saw significant impacts. This includes 50-70 homes and 20-25 businesses, one of which was a school in Menifee County, being inundated with water. Numerous roads were damaged as culverts were washed out, forcing schools to close the following day. Disaster declarations were made for Montgomery and Menifee Counties while nearly a dozen water rescues were performed. Additionally, a nine year old boy was swept into a culvert in Morehead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was observed rushing across Adams Ridges Road from Brush Creek just west of Clay City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 71 was closed in both directions in Oldham County due to high water and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported Georgetown Road shut down between Iron Works Pike and Berea Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted the region on the last day of May. Damaging winds, large hail, and flooding were observed in this outbreak. The first round of storms moved through northwest Kentucky, mainly north of Madisonville, during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Locally damaging winds were associated with this line of storms. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. The first round of storms left an outflow boundary across southeast Missouri and the lower Ohio Valley, where scattered thunderstorms developed. Surface dewpoints near the boundary were in the lower 70s, which contributed to moderately strong instability. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the late day and evening round of storms. Mid-level winds around 50 knots located just ahead of the shortwave helped to create moderate to strong deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Highway 91 about four miles northwest of Hopkinsville, a tree was down and corn was laying flat. Trees were blown down three miles north of Hopkinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported water rescues near Oxmoor Mall on Shelbyville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two mesoscale convective systems moved through eastern Kentucky during the day. The first tracked through during the early to mid afternoon hours, while the second followed in its wake from mid afternoon through the evening. Strong to severe winds along the leading edge of each complex led to scattered damage throughout eastern Kentucky, mainly consisting of downed trees. Additionally, isolated flash flooding occurred in Jackson and Whitley Counties due to the multiple rounds of heavy rainfall. Clover Bottom in particular saw very heavy rainfall as over 3 inches of rain in a short period of time damaged a portion of U.S. Highway 421.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained Skywarn Storm Spotter observed portions of U.S. Highway 421, from Sandgap to Clover Bottom, heavily damaged from rapidly flowing flood waters following over three inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 64 exit at 3rd Street closed due to high water from Ohio River flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow-moving warm front moved northeast across western Kentucky during the daytime hours. The air mass along and behind the front was moist and unstable, setting the stage for locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Hendron community, a basement collapsed due to excessive runoff down a hillside. A vehicle water rescue was conducted in Paducah, within a few miles of the Hendron incident. A Paducah city street intersection was blocked to traffic, and spotty road closures were reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported high water and flooding on Kieth Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down on Highway 70 near Mammoth Cave National Park blocking both sides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave swung through the Tennessee Valley causing widespread rainfall, with a deformation band of heavy rain near the Ohio River in central Kentucky. This caused areas of flash flooding in Jefferson County, KY and severe weather in Clinton County, KY. The rainfall set a record for the most rain in Louisville on the day of the Kentucky Derby. The record of 3.15 broke the old record of 2.31 set May 11, 1918. The daily record for May 5th was also broken. The old record of 2.14 was set in 2003.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The off ramp from Interstate 65 northbound to Interstate 264 westbound was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two mesoscale convective systems moved through eastern Kentucky during the day. The first tracked through during the early to mid afternoon hours, while the second followed in its wake from mid afternoon through the evening. Strong to severe winds along the leading edge of each complex led to scattered damage throughout eastern Kentucky, mainly consisting of downed trees. Additionally, isolated flash flooding occurred in Jackson and Whitley Counties due to the multiple rounds of heavy rainfall. Clover Bottom in particular saw very heavy rainfall as over 3 inches of rain in a short period of time damaged a portion of U.S. Highway 421.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch relayed a report of a tree blown down on Bluestone Road west of Morehead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the warmth of the afternoon. A weak mid-level shortwave trough over the Ozarks helped initiate convection. Although the air mass was only marginally unstable, thunderstorms were able to organize within a veering and modestly strengthening wind profile. Deep layer bulk wind shear was around 30 to 35 knots. Multicell storm clusters produced isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter on U.S. Highway 68 near Elkton reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure advanced from southern Michigan to the east with a co-located cold front that pushed through the Ohio Valley during the evening hours on May 22, 2018. Ahead of the front, a pool of warm (temperatures lifted into the 80s) and moist (precipitable water >= 1.5) atmosphere produced scattered showers and thunderstorms, causing water to flow out of a creek and over a roadway in Russell County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multiple day period of rainfall led to flooding and flash flooding across portions of southeast and far eastern Kentucky on this day. A cold front, which pushed into eastern Kentucky on September 26, stalled across southeast Kentucky and oozed back toward the northwest today. This brought another widespread round of 1-2 inches of rain across eastern Kentucky, with amounts exceeding 2.5 inches in Floyd and Harlan Counties. Along with numerous creeks and streams overspilling their banks and impacting roadways, several mudslides were induced by saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Rescue reported several locations along Kentucky Highways 404 and 850 southwest of Prestonsburg were flooded due to overflowing ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed this afternoon ahead of an upper level disturbance, bringing moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into eastern Kentucky. Isolated wind damage occurred in Laurel County as one storm initially grew very strong. Additional storms produced penny to nickel sized hail, while portions of northeast Letcher County experienced flash flooding due to persistent storms producing between 2-3 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch relayed a report of a large tree blown down on Kentucky Highway 490 near East Bernstadt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms developed from mid to late this afternoon across central Kentucky. As this line progressed east into the Bluegrass region of eastern Kentucky, these storms increased in coverage as rainfall rates increased. This led to instances of flash flooding across portions of Bath and Montgomery Counties. These storms continued to move east into the evening hours, creating additional flash flooding from Morehead to west of Sandy Hook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Management reported several roadways containing at least half a foot of flowing water near Flat Creek and west of Owingsville, leading to a vehicle becoming stranded on U.S. Highway 60 near the Montgomery County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Highway 1375 between mile post 2 and 4 closed due to high water along with Kentucky Highway 210 at mile post 4.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported water from Osborn Creek flowing across roadways east of Strunk and Pine Knot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of rainfall over the weekend and into early week occurred along a stationary frontal boundary. Portions of northwestern Fleming County received between 2.5 to 3.5 inches of rain during this time frame, which resulted in flooding north of Flemingsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported that Mill Creek Road and Pleasantville Road north of Flemingsburg were both closed due to standing water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multiple day period of rainfall led to flooding and flash flooding across portions of southeast and far eastern Kentucky on this day. A cold front, which pushed into eastern Kentucky on September 26, stalled across southeast Kentucky and oozed back toward the northwest today. This brought another widespread round of 1-2 inches of rain across eastern Kentucky, with amounts exceeding 2.5 inches in Floyd and Harlan Counties. Along with numerous creeks and streams overspilling their banks and impacting roadways, several mudslides were induced by saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Manager reported that a mudslide caused the shutdown of Calhoun drive in Prestonsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon merged with a cold front over the mid-section of the country. The tropical moisture squeezed out by the front produced widespread heavy rainfall. During the afternoon hours, there was enough solar heating and destabilization for thunderstorms to increase across western Kentucky. Enough low-level wind shear was present for a few rotating storms and tornadoes in the Owensboro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds in this tornado were estimated near 110 mph. The tornado skirted the southern and eastern parts of the small community of Stanley. There were several witnesses to this widely filmed and documented tornado. A well-defined, convergent damage path was evident in drone footage of the area. The east wall of one home was nearly completely destroyed. Roofs were torn off a few barns and sheds. Several homes received light siding and fascia damage. Corn and tobacco crops were flattened. Several dozen trees were uprooted or topped. The tornado reached its strongest point where it crossed U.S. Highway 60 at Stanley. This tornado was the strongest of four tornadoes that were spawned by a cluster of storms in the Owensboro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon merged with a cold front over the mid-section of the country. The tropical moisture squeezed out by the front produced widespread heavy rainfall. During the afternoon hours, there was enough solar heating and destabilization for thunderstorms to increase across western Kentucky. Enough low-level wind shear was present for a few rotating storms and tornadoes in the Owensboro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This weak tornado caused no property damage, however a path of crop damage was visible in drone footage. Peak winds were estimated near 80 mph. The tornado touched down on Highway 279 and continued northeast for about 0.6 miles to Highway 56. The tornado track was based on video and drone footage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds in this half-mile long tornado were estimated near 95 mph. The tornado touched down midway between the small communities of Maceo and Yelvington. A few trees were snapped, and there was minor soffit damage to a home. The damage survey was based upon drone footage and other pictures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe storms hammered southern Indiana and central Kentucky on Friday, July 20, 2018, bringing very large hail, wind damage, heavy rain, and 4 confirmed tornadoes. The largest hail report the National Weather Service received was 4 inches in diameter (softball size) in Tompkinsville, just north of the Tennessee border. The strongest measured wind gust was 70 mph at the Lexington Bluegrass Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southward into Illinois and Indiana. It triggered convection during the mid afternoon. Storms dropped into southern Indiana and north central Kentucky by the late afternoon and evening. High percipitable water values of 2 were waiting ahead of the front near the Ohio River. DCAPE values of around 1,000 J/kg also sat over the area providing conditions for heavy rain and severe thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees came down at Carpenter Pike and US 68. Trees also came down along county roads in the western half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many areas in and around West Point experienced significant river and areal flooding due to excessive rainfall. Multiple roads were washed out, eroded or severely flooded. Many bridges had cracks or broken joints.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in extreme northeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sandy River at Grayson surpassed its 21 foot flood stage just after midnight on the 10th. The river crested in minor flood stage at 22.6 feet just before sunrise and returned to its banks shortly after noon. This flooded sections of Route 773 in the Dixie Park area and SR 1910 was also flooded along with some farmland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in extreme northeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tygarts Creek near Grayson flooded on the 10th. The creek rose above its 20 foot flood stage just after noon. It crested at 20.7 feet in minor flood stage that evening and returned within its banks shortly after midnight on the 11th. This flooded low areas along SR 7 and CR 2070. The country story and some homes near the creek in the town of Load also had minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm damage survey in Logan county Kentucky. The damage moved in from Robertson County Tennessee near the end of McGee Road where two homes received extensive damage. The first home, a well built stone home had significant roof damage and the screened in porch was destroyed. The family pick up truck was tossed around 40 yards to the north. On the family cemetery plot several tombstones were knocked over and one headstone was destroyed. There were several outbuildings and 2 pole Barnes were destroyed on the property as well. ||The second home experienced significant foundation damage and the roof was completely thrown off. The family pick up truck was thrown 250 yards and thrown down into a field. Insulation from the home was thrown down wind over 300 yards. The debris field from the two homes was thrown in a farmers field between 100 yards in a quarter mile down wind with several 2 x 4 and 2 x 10 and bricks being impaled into the ground.  Winds were estimated between 120 and 130 mph. ||The tornado then moved through several farmers fields doing extensive tree damage and fence damage. Power lines were down throughout this entire stretch.| |The tornado then hit 901 Dot Road destroying 2 barns, moving heavy farm equipment, blowing out exterior walls and destroying the roof at the property. Tragically, a woman in the home was killed by falling debris.  The family pet dog was found alive by rescuers underneath extensive rubble.  Debris was thrown up to 500 yards and debris with a width of between 250 and and 300 yards in a farmers field.  Winds were estimated to be 135 mph at this location. ||Next, the tornado hit a large family farm at 3604 Schley Road destroying the second story. Three farm silos that were empty or destroyed with the metal sheets being thrown over a quarter mile down wind. The tornado continued through farm fields destroying several trees and uprooting them.||The tornado became more elevated at the intersection of Mortimor Station Road and Mirriaheh Road.  In this area trees we snapped, and several homes experienced shingle siding and roof damage. Winds were estimated to be 100 to 110 mph here. ||As the tornado was lifting, it snapped some trees in the Schochoh community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe storms hammered southern Indiana and central Kentucky on Friday, July 20, 2018, bringing very large hail, wind damage, heavy rain, and 4 confirmed tornadoes. The largest hail report the National Weather Service received was 4 inches in diameter (softball size) in Tompkinsville, just north of the Tennessee border. The strongest measured wind gust was 70 mph at the Lexington Bluegrass Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was widespread tree damage along Burgin Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado touched down east of a row of trees, where it caused roof damage to 2 out of 3 barns. The tornado headed east, where it downed 2 other barns. Tall grass along a fence line within the width of the tornado showed evidence of convergence.  Also, a large tree on the north side of the path was snapped southward and drug 10 feet south. Heading east, several trees fell near a residence, but none damaged the house itself. A detached garage had its door blown outward.  Next, a 2 story house had some roof damage and a porch on the lee side of the house had a column fly out down a field about 50 yards. Lastly, the tornado struck a barn and several trees fell. At this residence, a carport was thrown over the house and landed 100 yards east. A few |other trees fell beyond this point before the tornado lifted.

The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt River at Shepherdsville went into moderate flood stage. The river crested at 35.01 feet which is 3.01 feet above flood stage.

By September 8th, Gordon was centered in southeast Missouri with much of its moisture and energy in the Ohio River Valley interacted with a stationary front. On September 5, 2018, the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon moved north from the Gulf of Mexico. It carried copious amounts of moisture as it continued to rotate while slowly moving north.  In Kentucky, the outer bands of the system brought showers and thunderstorms that produced isolated wind damage September 5 and 6.   By September 8th, Gordon was centered in southeast Missouri with much of its moisture and energy in the Ohio River Valley interacted with a stationary front. This produced efficient rainfall that caused flash flooding, and the environmental shear was strong enough to produce two tornadoes along the Ohio River in southern Indiana and north-central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tree-top level tornado did all of its damage 20 to 30 feet above ground. Never more than 50 yards wide, the first evidence of the tornado touching down was one mile south of the water tower on Thomas Lane, where it downed several large branches that blocked the road. Moving northeast over forested land, the next evidence was more tree trunks and branches snapped on Poplar Grove Rd just north of the intersection with Hilldale Rd. The tornado then continued to skip northeast, snapping branches and trunks to the west of Frank Luttrell Rd, before crossing the road and causing minor roof damage to the metal roof of a barn and snapping more tree limbs a third of a mile southeast of North Hancock Elementary School. The tornado dropped small branches and leaves along its path as it continued northeast. The last major tree damage was observed around a home on Adair Rd, where trees were uprooted and trunks snapped in a cyclonic pattern around the home. The only damage to the home, however, was minor roof damage from a branch falling on it. The storm then crossed Adair Rd, lifting somewhere in a wooded area west of a large pond near Beauchamp Lane, where residents witnessed its dissipation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of September 22nd, a flash flood watch was issued to cover southeast Indiana and areas in and around the Bluegrass Region of Kentucky.||By September 23rd, a cold front stretched west to east from western Tennessee to far |east-central Kentucky on through eastern Kentucky. The front was tilted northward with the 700 mb level of the front just north of the Ohio River. Low level northward flow carried moisture and overran the front producing widespread stratiform rain showers. The pattern was in response to the area sitting under the right entrance region of an upper level jet.||The quasi-stationary front produced showers with high rainfall rates and slow movement to the east into the Bluegrass and northeastern Kentucky. The ground east of Interstate 65 and in counties along the Ohio River was already saturated from previous rainfall. The high rainfall rates and slow system movement caused widespread flash flooding and areal flooding through southern Indiana and the Bluegrass Region of Kentucky.||On September 25th ahead of an advancing cold front, a saturated air mass was in place with precipitable water values near two inches. With this air mass and shear ahead of the cold front, convective showers formed over southern Indiana and advanced east along Interstate 65 causing flooding issues in the system's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Grays Run Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in extreme northeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding along Sandsuck Creek closed a section of Route 207. Three Prong Branch flooded, closing Three Prong Road. Big Lost Creek Road was closed due to high water along Lost Creek. High water also closed the intersection of Route 7 and Route 784.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central Kentucky sat on the western side of a strong upper ridge, but as a trough moved in from the west, it caused severe weather and flooding issues on the 2nd. High precipitable water values around 2 and lingering boundaries caused heavy rain and flooding issues on the 3rd as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local TV station showed video of Lexington Road flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was water over parts of Highway 421 and other rural roads southeast of Bedford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe storms hammered southern Indiana and central Kentucky on Friday, July 20, 2018, bringing very large hail, wind damage, heavy rain, and 4 confirmed tornadoes. The largest hail report the National Weather Service received was 4 inches in diameter (softball size) in Tompkinsville, just north of the Tennessee border. The strongest measured wind gust was 70 mph at the Lexington Bluegrass Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on US 25 and Interstate 75 near mile marker 99.

Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water over road and bridge at Old Heady and Veech Road.

Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stoner Creek at Paris went into minor flood. The river crested at 18.88 on February 22, which is 0.88 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms developed in the afternoon over central Kentucky. This weather was triggered by an upper level shortwave pushing in from the southwest and a weak surface boundary over south central Kentucky that was drifting northward.  While organized severe weather did not develop, very heavy rainfall caused the death of a 15 year old boy. He was playing near a drainage ditch in his back yard with his brother when flowing water pushed him through a culvert. He died in the hospital a week later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15-year old and his brother were playing near an open drainage ditch running through their back yard near the 9800 block of Melissa Drive when he slipped and fell into the rapidly running water.  He was swept 100 yards through a culvert to the 9400 block of Tamarisk Drive.  While he survived being submersed in the culvert, he never regained consciousness and passed away on September 7th.  Approximately 1.5 of rain fell in the area, about an inch of it between 5:15 and 6:15 pm EDT. The boy slipped into the ditch around 7 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down in a wooded area midway between J Riggs Rd and Copelin Rd north of state highway 728, snapping trees as it moved southeast along a hay field where it destroyed two 1200 lb hay bales and rolled numerous others up to a tenth of a mile into a soybean field and uphill onto a knoll. Snapping trees on the land adjacent to the hay and soybean fields, the tornado took a slight right turn, crossing highway 728, and destroyed a large barn.  Large parts of the barn debris were lofted into trees southwest of the structure, snapping their trunks.  A 20 x 20 section of the barn was blown 500 feet to the south-southeast.  The tornado continued southward for another two-tenths of a mile, downing trees along Campground Rd before lifting.  Maximum winds with this tornado were estimated to be 90 to 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe storms hammered southern Indiana and central Kentucky on Friday, July 20, 2018, bringing very large hail, wind damage, heavy rain, and 4 confirmed tornadoes. The largest hail report the National Weather Service received was 4 inches in diameter (softball size) in Tompkinsville, just north of the Tennessee border. The strongest measured wind gust was 70 mph at the Lexington Bluegrass Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short-lived spin-up occurred as a northeast-moving storm interacted with outflow winds from a southeastward-moving storm in eastern Metcalfe County. The first damage occurred along Subtle Road, where a carport was overturned and a large part of a metal outbuilding's roof was torn off, in addition to snapping numerous trees.  The tornado then moved east-northeast at tree-top level over wooded areas and farm fields, doing its next major damage a mile away where it pushed a double-wide mobile home to the northwest off its blocks, breaking windows, and ripping off shingles from the roof. The tornado continued on for another mile, causing occasional tree damage, and dropping debris before lifting near the east end of Groce Road, where it peeled roofing material from a metal out building.  Maximum wind speeds with this tornado were estimated at 80 to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southward into Illinois and Indiana. It triggered convection during the mid afternoon. Storms dropped into southern Indiana and north central Kentucky by the late afternoon and evening. High percipitable water values of 2 were waiting ahead of the front near the Ohio River. DCAPE values of around 1,000 J/kg also sat over the area providing conditions for heavy rain and severe thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down on a power line at Shawnee Estates in Bowling Green.

Some storms reached severe intensities and caused trees to blow down in other parts of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a few limbs down along Raikes Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short bowing line of thunderstorms moved east across southwest Indiana and northwest Kentucky. The southern end of this line of storms crossed northwest Kentucky, causing pockets of wind damage to trees and power lines. The storms occurred ahead of a warm front that extended southeast from a low pressure center over northwest Missouri. The storms occurred along a corridor of stronger low level winds, averaging near 30 knots at 1 km agl. Steep low level lapse rates allowed strong winds to reach the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Owensboro. Trees were down several miles southeast of St. Joseph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms gradually evolved into a bowing line of storms as it crossed southern Illinois. Locally damaging winds accompanied the bowing line of storms as it continued eastward across northwest Kentucky. The storms increased in intensity as a result of strong heating and destabilization during the late morning hours. This round of storms was followed by another round of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the corner of Highways 815 and 140 in northern Mclean County, a camper trailer at least 20 feet long was overturned onto its roof. Numerous trees were blown down nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across southeast Kentucky late this afternoon into this evening, with one becoming severe across portions of northern Bell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported a large tree blown down along Kentucky Highway 221 near Stoney Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms brought periods of heavy rainfall to portions of southeast Kentucky from this morning into this evening, leading to instances of flooding and flash flooding. The worst flash flooding occurred in Harlan County, particularly near the Ages to Jones Creek area where a few locales received greater than 3 inches of rain in just over a 24 hour period. Late morning and afternoon heating allowed for a few storms to become severe, producing damaging wind gusts from early to mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported flooding on Montgomery Creek Road in Vicco, as well as on Kentucky Highway 15 southeast of Hazard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunny skies led to strong surface heating, with temperatures in the lower 90's. The hot and very moist air mass contributed to very strong instability. lsolated thunderstorms formed along and ahead of an outflow boundary from the remnants of morning thunderstorms. Isolated large hail fell from these afternoon storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported on the south and southwest sides of Murray.

Afternoon heat indices peaked between 100 and 105 degrees on three consecutive days from the 16th to the 18th. Actual air temperatures reached the lower to mid 90's. When these temperatures were combined with dew points in the mid 70's, the heat index surpassed the century mark. A strong 500 mb high was centered over the lower Ohio and middle Mississippi Valleys during this heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the Lake Cumberland region and Interstate 75 corridor late this afternoon and early evening ahead of an approaching upper level disturbance. Instability became great enough in order for these storms to briefly develop robust updrafts, which later descended thus leading to isolated wind damage across McCreary and Whitley Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch relayed a report of a tree blown down onto a power line and garage near Honeybee, resulting in a structural fire.

Numerous thunderstorms developed in the heat of the afternoon south of an outflow boundary that was draped across central Illinois and east central Missouri. Vertical wind shear was very weak, resulting in little if any storm organization. Even so, a few storms briefly pulsed up to severe levels. Damaging wind gusts were very isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down in the community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A loosely organized band of thunderstorms developed over southern Illinois during the heat of the afternoon, and then moved southeast into western Kentucky. The strongest storm cell within this band produced an isolated damaging wind event. A surface front was reinforced and pushed south near the Ohio River from overnight and morning convection. One of the early morning storms produced a damaging lightning strike. The afternoon storms developed along this boundary stretching from eastern Kentucky back to southeastern Missouri. Weak winds aloft kept the activity rather limited in terms of intensity and organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Calloway County fairgrounds on Highway 121 west of Murray, carnival rides were damaged and several tents were blown down. At Almo, trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multi-cell clusters of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon and early evening. The strongest cluster of storms tracked from just east of Paducah across the Kentucky Lake region to the Hopkinsville area. Sporadic reports of wind damage were received with this cluster of storms. The storms occurred along an eastward-moving cold front. The environment ahead of the cold front was very moist and moderately unstable, supportive of intense convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered tree damage occurred in northern Marshall County, associated with a severe thunderstorm. Numerous trees were blown down from the community of Palma to Kentucky Dam. A portable building was moved off its foundation in Palma. Several large tree limbs were down in Calvert City. Power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorm cells tracked southeast from Illinois into western Kentucky during the evening hours. The storms were enhanced by lift aloft associated with a mid to upper level disturbance over the mid Mississippi Valley. Instability increased with southward extent into the Ohio Valley, supporting the intensification of the storms. Wind shear profiles supported storm organization, which contributed to a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud briefly touched down on the outskirts of Calvert City. A few tree limbs were down.

Scattered showers and storms consolidated into a couple of very strong thunderstorms as they moved east across western Kentucky. An isolated large hail and damaging wind event occurred. The storms developed along an outflow boundary from morning convection draped from central Arkansas into parts of southwest Kentucky. An upper level disturbance over southeastern Missouri moved east, helping to strengthen the storms. Moderately strong deep-layer wind shear and moderate instability supported isolated damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole was snapped, causing a power outage. Pieces of a roof were blown off a pole barn garage. Several tree limbs were down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms gradually evolved into a bowing line of storms as it crossed southern Illinois. Locally damaging winds accompanied the bowing line of storms as it continued eastward across northwest Kentucky. The storms increased in intensity as a result of strong heating and destabilization during the late morning hours. This round of storms was followed by another round of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 63 mph was measured at the Kentucky mesonet site. A power line was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in east central Missouri during the afternoon along a remnant outflow boundary from the morning thunderstorms. The afternoon storms moved southeast and intensified in the unstable air along the slowing outflow boundary. Afternoon heating contributed to moderately strong instability. Rather strong wind shear resulted in some organized lines and bowing lines of storms. Sporadic damaging winds occurred in association with the lines. Two lines of storms slowed down and merged over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky, causing very heavy rain that produced some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten trees were down along U.S. Highway 41A. One of the trees fell on a car, causing injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms moved southeast, producing isolated severe wind gusts. The storms occurred in the vicinity of a quasi-stationary front extending from northern Missouri southeastward into western Kentucky. The air mass was quite unstable, with mixed layer cape values in the 2000 to 3500 J/kg range. Deep layer wind shear was just enough for a few severe multi-cells to occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm complex raced southward across western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Widespread wind damage occurred with this long-lived complex, which met the definition of a derecho. This convective system took shape over the Missouri/Iowa border region and then progressed south-southeast to the Tennessee Valley by late evening. A very moist and unstable air mass set the stage for this derecho. Surface dew points were near 80 degrees, with mixed-layer capes over 4000 j/kg. When the atmosphere is that unstable, wind shear does not need to be particularly strong for significant severe weather to occur. The bulk wind shear in the 0 to 6 km layer averaged 20 to 30 knots. Deep northerly wind flow averaged around 20 knots from 850 to 500 mb on the front side of a high pressure ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds occurred across the county. Numerous trees and power lines were down. Near Burna, several trees were uprooted on Highway 60 near the intersection of Highway 135. Seven miles east of Smithland, a damage survey conducted by NWS personnel confirmed several large trees were snapped or uprooted by winds near 75 mph. Wooden building materials were scattered across the front yard of a residence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds occurred across much of Ballard County. Numerous large trees were blown down, resulting in widespread power outages. A tree landed on a car. Another tree landed on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm complex raced southward across western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Widespread wind damage occurred with this long-lived complex, which met the definition of a derecho. This convective system took shape over the Missouri/Iowa border region and then progressed south-southeast to the Tennessee Valley by late evening. A very moist and unstable air mass set the stage for this derecho. Surface dew points were near 80 degrees, with mixed-layer capes over 4000 j/kg. When the atmosphere is that unstable, wind shear does not need to be particularly strong for significant severe weather to occur. The bulk wind shear in the 0 to 6 km layer averaged 20 to 30 knots. Deep northerly wind flow averaged around 20 knots from 850 to 500 mb on the front side of a high pressure ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds occurred over much of the county. Three state roads were closed by downed trees. A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 60 mph at Eddyville.

These temperatures, combined with dew points in the mid 70's, produced hazardous conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A loosely organized band of thunderstorms developed over southern Illinois during the heat of the afternoon, and then moved southeast into western Kentucky. The strongest storm cell within this band produced an isolated damaging wind event. A surface front was reinforced and pushed south near the Ohio River from overnight and morning convection. One of the early morning storms produced a damaging lightning strike. The afternoon storms developed along this boundary stretching from eastern Kentucky back to southeastern Missouri. Weak winds aloft kept the activity rather limited in terms of intensity and organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two houses were struck by lightning and on fire.

An area of disorganized thunderstorms over southwest Indiana evolved into a line of strong to severe storms as it moved southeast across the Ohio Valley. The storms evolved along the leading edge of outflow winds from an overnight thunderstorm complex. The storms were displaced to the south of the stronger winds aloft, limiting their severe weather potential. A few of the stronger cells produced damaging winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down.

As a cold front sunk through the middle Ohio River Valley, showers and thunderstorms moved through northeastern Kentucky late on the 11th into early on the 12th. With abundant moisture in the atmosphere these showers and storms produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water closed a portion of Rupert Lane near the Grayson city limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms consolidated into a couple of very strong thunderstorms as they moved east across western Kentucky. An isolated large hail and damaging wind event occurred. The storms developed along an outflow boundary from morning convection draped from central Arkansas into parts of southwest Kentucky. An upper level disturbance over southeastern Missouri moved east, helping to strengthen the storms. Moderately strong deep-layer wind shear and moderate instability supported isolated damaging winds and large hail.

Thunderstorms occurred along a cold front as it moved east-southeast across the Ohio Valley. Although deep-layer wind shear was rather weak, there was enough lingering instability for isolated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department responded to downed trees and power lines in Stanley. In Sorgho, a tree was snapped and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in the heat of the afternoon south of an outflow boundary that was draped across central Illinois and east central Missouri. Vertical wind shear was very weak, resulting in little if any storm organization. Even so, a few storms briefly pulsed up to severe levels. Damaging wind gusts were very isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees and power lines were blown down from the Central City area to near Drakesboro. About 4 miles south of Central City, one tree fell on a mobile home, injuring two people. The injuries were not serious. Numerous trees were down along U.S. Highway 431.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in east central Missouri during the afternoon along a remnant outflow boundary from the morning thunderstorms. The afternoon storms moved southeast and intensified in the unstable air along the slowing outflow boundary. Afternoon heating contributed to moderately strong instability. Rather strong wind shear resulted in some organized lines and bowing lines of storms. Sporadic damaging winds occurred in association with the lines. Two lines of storms slowed down and merged over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky, causing very heavy rain that produced some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs were blown down. A small tree landed on a house. Shingles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm complex raced southward across western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Widespread wind damage occurred with this long-lived complex, which met the definition of a derecho. This convective system took shape over the Missouri/Iowa border region and then progressed south-southeast to the Tennessee Valley by late evening. A very moist and unstable air mass set the stage for this derecho. Surface dew points were near 80 degrees, with mixed-layer capes over 4000 j/kg. When the atmosphere is that unstable, wind shear does not need to be particularly strong for significant severe weather to occur. The bulk wind shear in the 0 to 6 km layer averaged 20 to 30 knots. Deep northerly wind flow averaged around 20 knots from 850 to 500 mb on the front side of a high pressure ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds occurred over the county. Numerous trees and power lines were down. Some roads were blocked by downed trees and power lines, especially Highway 2187 on the south side of Paducah. The roof was partially torn off a downtown office building in Paducah, and windows were blown out. A power pole was down in Paducah. Thousands of people lost power. Some of the power outages lasted through the following day. The peak wind gust at the National Weather Service at Barkley Regional Airport was 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, developed ahead of cold front and caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cars throughout the county were stuck in high water. First responders were called out to assist, but nothing was life threatening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with warm moist air aloft moved through the region producing a wintery mix of primarily freezing rain, with some sleet and snow mixed in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CVG airport measured 0.18 inches of ice accrual. Numerous travel difficulties occurred across the region due to icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved southeast from the northern Plains into the lower Ohio Valley. Clear skies and light winds set the stage for widespread frost and a brief period of sub-freezing temperatures. Low temperatures were around 30 degrees. The growing season was ended by this first freeze of the season.

Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 362 closed due to high water and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Overbrook Driver closed due to high water and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River rose above flood stage late in the month. December ended up mild and wet. Total precipitation amounts for the month were up to 2.5 inches above normal. Record daily precipitation amounts were noted on the 1st and 14th at several locations. It was the heavy rain at mid-month that caused the river to rise above flood stage. The river remained above flood stage into the new year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River rose above flood stage. Minor flooding caused officials to close the Ballard Wildlife Management Area, which consists of large tracts of wetland formed by Ohio River sloughs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and fields near the river were inundated. A few access roads to farms were underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary interacting with highly anomalous moisture from the Gulf of Mexico interacted with multiple weather systems that brought widespread heavy rainfall and severe thunderstorms to central Kentucky February 24 through the early morning hours February 25. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Jefferson County, KY including Louisville metro. There were over 50 water rescues alone in Jefferson County that evening. Widespread flash flooding occurred with most streams and creeks reaching record levels. ||In addition, across south central Kentucky, 7 tornadoes developed along a warm front during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 that tracked over 11 miles in Logan County and tragically killed a woman in her home. As a squall line moved through during the late evening and early morning hours, damaging winds occurred across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and brief EF1 tornado touched down near  a ridge-top on Gillespie Pike east of Lancaster.  The tornado first uprooted a tree, then came to a small shed, which it shifted off its foundation into a fence.  The main property for that shed then had a loss of roof covering as well as impact from debris. Another outbuilding was overturned and slid about 10 feet. The tornado then crossed the street, where a brick veneer home lost part of its outer wall, as it was struck by debris, as well as some roof damage.  A couple of columns on the porch fell. The adjoining garage lost its roof and doors were bent inward, falling onto the vehicles inside.  A barn behind the house collapsed and another outbuilding completely lost its walls, but the contents were not disturbed. This is the point where the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers became heavier and more numerous during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of the snow showers reduced visibility to around one mile. Most of the activity was east of Kentucky Lake, including the Pennyrile region and Owensboro and Henderson areas. The snow accumulated up to an inch in some spots. A trained spotter reported one inch of accumulation two miles north of Hopkinsville. A co-operative observer in southern Henderson County measured one-half inch. A slushy coating on roads was sufficient to cause some traffic accidents. Traffic accidents were reported in Muhlenberg County, including a collision between a car and a snow plow on U.S. Highway 431. Even after the snow ended, the combination of wet roads from earlier snow and sub-freezing temperatures created patchy slick spots during the early morning commute on the 5th. Schools were closed on the 5th in Muhlenberg and Todd Counties. A couple of other counties delayed the opening of schools by two hours. Ahead of high pressure over the Plains states, a moist and unstable northwest wind flow generated numerous snow showers. The snow showers were enhanced by 500 mb shortwaves rotating east around a low over the upper Mississippi Valley.

A low pressure system moved east across Mississippi and Alabama, spreading a wintry mix across southern parts of western Kentucky. The primary type of precipitation was freezing rain, which glazed elevated surfaces with around one-tenth inch of ice. Most road surfaces stayed wet. The icing was mainly along and south of a line from Clinton to Mayfield, Benton, and Cadiz, as well as Christian and Todd Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm survey across Simpson County. The tornado began just southwest of Lake Spring Road where a couple of trees were snapped or uprooted and a large 24 x 36 barn experienced extensive roof panel damage.  Maximum winds were estimated at 80 mph with a width between 75 and 100 yards.||The tornado contnued to cross farmland before striking another barn near the 31W intersection. The tornado threw metal sheeting from the barn in various directions with much of it suspended up in the trees. There were a couple twisted trees on both sides of Highway 31W.  Maximum winds at this location were 75 mph with a width of 75 yards. The tornado lifted on the northeast side of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of showers and thunderstorms raced east-northeast across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana, producing pockets of damaging wind and a brief tornado. A deepening low pressure system tracked northeast from Arkansas across the St. Louis area to Indiana. To the east of this low, a warm front lifted north across the lower Ohio Valley in the morning, followed by unseasonably warm and moist air. A trailing cold front then moved east, triggering a narrow line of showers and thunderstorms along the Mississippi River by midday. This line of convection became sporadically severe as it raced across western Kentucky in the afternoon. Heavy rainfall caused some isolated flooding of creeks and roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service determined that a microburst affected a small area off Interstate 24, just east of exit 3. The damage started four miles west-northwest of downtown Paducah, just east of Interstate 24 on Highway 305. Peak winds of up to 80 mph stripped metal siding or roofing from at least three businesses and one home. An hvac unit atop a large big-box retail store was dislodged. Metal fencing was blown off a business. Several trees or tree limbs were broken. Two power poles were blown over. The damage ended just north of the intersection of Highways 305 and 1420, about 3.5 miles northwest of downtown Paducah. Most of the damage was along and north of Highway 305 and east of I-24.

There was significant damage to a barn on Kentucky Highway 994 across from the North Graves Fire Department. At least two outbuildings were damaged, and a detached garage was destroyed. Several trees or tree limbs were broken.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-1 tornado touched down just offshore in Lake Barkley near Blue Water Estates. The tornado tracked eastward through part of the subdivision, damaging at least six homes. The damage was minor, consisting mostly of loss of shingles and vinyl siding. There were some broken windows and screens blown in. Three garage doors were blown inward, and one garage lost shingles. A couple of dozen trees were snapped or uprooted. There were two separate eyewitness reports of a tornado. Peak winds were estimated near 90 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the intersection of U.S. Highway 431 and County Road 250, numerous trees were down. A small outbuilding was destroyed when its cinder block walls collapsed. A small wooden storage shed about 20 by 20 feet was blown about 50 feet off its foundation. Windows were blown out of a modular home, and minor damage occurred to a wall of the home. Plastic and metal pipes were blown about one-quarter mile into a field. Softwood trees were snapped 15 to 30 feet off the ground. A pine tree landed on the roof of a house, causing damage to the metal roof. A couple of farm outbuildings sustained minor damage, including a chicken house. Peak winds were estimated near 85 mph. The damage path was about one mile long and up to one-quarter mile wide. Most of the damage was along County Road 250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across central Kentucky from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. The Ohio River along the Indiana and Kentucky border rose into Moderate to Major flood stage. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the central Kentucky counties including road closures, road washouts, water rescues, and 1 flood fatality in Simpson County. Kentucky Emergency Management reported damages to public infrastructure exceeding $3.5 Million reported from nine central KY counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 111 flooded in several areas.

This cold weather was responsible for causing a woman to die as she was exposed to these temperatures early in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local media outlet reported that a woman died while being exposed to cold weather early this morning.
